_patch_meta.session_ref: M04 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 77 verses across 3 terms

set.analysis_note: The quality of being pleasant here is an aesthetic and sensory one — trees are pleasing to the eye, stirring a perceptual delight in the beholder through their visible beauty.
Pleasantness here is a quality of material excellence perceived in gold, bdellium, and onyx — desirable resources whose worth registers as something finely appealing.
The daughters of man are seen as attractive — their visual appeal stirs desire in the sons of God, prompting deliberate choice and action toward them.
A 'good old age' frames the end of life as a satisfying, full, and peaceful experience — the pleasantness is the inner sense of completeness and peace at death's approach.
The calf is described as tender and good — the pleasantness is a quality of physical excellence that makes it fitting and desirable for hospitality and feasting.
Rebekah's attractiveness is a perceived visual quality — her appearance makes a favorable impression, which is the outer form through which pleasantness is registered here.

Here 'good' is paired with 'bad' as the full range of human speech or interference — the verse does not evidence inner delight but rather the absence of any evaluative response permitted to Laban and Bethuel.
Abraham's 'good old age' conveys a life brought to satisfying fullness — the pleasantness is the inner sense of completion and contentment that attends a well-lived life at its close.

Rebekah's attractive appearance is the quality that generates fear in Isaac — her pleasantness to the sight is powerful enough to threaten his life, showing how deeply it registers in others' perception and desire.
The goats are 'good' in the sense of being fine, choice animals — pleasantness here is a quality of material excellence that makes them suitable for producing delicious food.
Leah experiences a 'good endowment' from God in having borne six sons — the pleasantness is her inner sense of being favored and honored, expected to produce relational warmth from her husband.
God warns Laban not to speak anything to Jacob, 'good or bad' — the verse does not evidence inner delight; 'good' here covers the full spectrum of speech, and inner-being content is absent.
As in the prior warning, 'good or bad' spans all possible speech directed at Jacob — the verse does not evidence inner joy or delight; it uses 'good' as one pole of a merism for total restraint.
The seven ears of grain are described as plump and good — pleasantness here is a quality of agricultural abundance and excellence perceived in the dream, signaling prosperous years ahead.
The seven ears are 'full and good' in the dream — pleasantness is again a quality of visible abundance, pointing inwardly to the anticipation of plenty that such a vision would stir.
The seven good ears consumed by thin ones picture the loss of abundance — the pleasantness of the good ears serves as the foil for the coming destruction, making the contrast felt.
The seven good cows and seven good ears both represent seven years of plenty — pleasantness here is the quality of fruitful abundance that the future holds, as perceived in the dream's imagery.
The 'good years' are years of agricultural abundance — pleasantness here is the quality of generous provision that must be gathered and stored, a time whose enjoyment depends on wise stewardship.

Issachar sees the resting place as good and the land as pleasant — his perception of these favorable conditions moves his will to accept servitude, showing how pleasantness shapes inner judgment and decision.

The mother sees that her newborn son is fine — her perception of the child's pleasing quality moves her to risk hiding him, showing how the sense of goodness and delight in what is seen drives protective action.
The promised land is described as good and broad — its pleasantness is an objective quality of abundance and desirability that motivates God's saving intervention and stirs hope in the people.
In ritual substitution law, 'good for bad' describes the quality differential of animals — the verse uses pleasantness as a standard of quality assessment rather than evidencing inner delight.
The priest evaluates an animal as either good or bad for vow purposes — pleasantness here is a quality judgment used in sacred valuation, not an inner emotional state.
The priest values a dedicated house as good or bad — pleasantness functions as a standard of quality in priestly assessment, with no inner-being emotional content evidenced in this verse.
The law forbids distinguishing between good and bad animals in tithing — pleasantness here is a quality distinction that is deliberately suspended, with no inner delight content in view.
The spies are sent to assess whether the land is good or bad — pleasantness here is a quality of the land to be perceived and reported, informing the people's sense of what awaits them.
Caleb and Joshua declare the land exceedingly good — their inner conviction that the land is desirable and excellent is expressed with enthusiastic confidence meant to move the congregation's will.
The scouts return fruit and report the land is good — the pleasantness is a quality they perceived that was meant to fill the people with confidence and eager desire for the gift.

The good land is withheld from the rebellious generation — pleasantness here is the desirable quality of the promised inheritance whose loss constitutes judgment, contrasting against the inner state of rebellion.
Moses pleads to see the good land — his longing reveals that the land's pleasantness has become an object of deep personal desire in his inner being, and its sight is a hoped-for delight.

Moses is barred from entering the good land because of Israel's sin — the land's pleasantness is the inheritance he is denied, making its goodness the measure of what divine anger costs him personally.

Moses contrasts his dying in this land with Israel going over to possess the good land — the pleasantness of that land is the gift Israel will enjoy while Moses is excluded, sharpening the pathos of his situation.
God brings Israel into the land with great and good cities — the pleasantness is an experienced quality of ready-made abundance that Israel will inhabit as unearned gift, meant to evoke gratitude.
God is bringing Israel into a good land rich with water — the pleasantness is the land's abundant, life-sustaining quality, meant to stir grateful recognition of divine generosity.

Building good houses is listed among the blessings that may breed forgetfulness of God — the pleasantness of fine dwellings is a comfort that can quietly displace the inner orientation toward gratitude.
Perishing from the good land is the consequence of turning away from God — the land's pleasantness is the precious possession at stake, its loss serving as the severest warning against disobedience.

The Lord opens his good treasury to give rain — the pleasantness of God's heavenly storehouse is the quality of generous, life-giving provision that flows from obedience, meant to produce grateful flourishing.
Achan sees a beautiful cloak and covets it — the object's visual pleasantness acted on his inner perception, arousing desire that overrode conscience and led to theft.
Perishing from the good ground is the threatened consequence of unfaithfulness — the land's pleasantness is what makes expulsion from it so grievous, functioning as a motivator for covenant loyalty.
Gideon dies in a good old age — the pleasantness is a life brought to satisfying fullness, marking his death as a fitting and honorable close to a long and significant life.
The fig tree prizes its sweetness and good fruit over ruling the trees — the pleasantness of its fruit is its valued identity, and attachment to that good quality drives its inner refusal of power.
The younger sister is said to be more beautiful than her sibling — the verse presents pleasantness as a comparative quality of appearance used to justify a relational substitution.
The scouts declare the land very good, urging immediate action — their inner perception of the land's desirability generates urgency and will to possess it without delay.
The king will take the best fields and vineyards — pleasantness here is the quality of prime, most desirable land that royal power will seize, turning a blessing into an instrument of oppression.
The king will take the best young men — their quality of excellence and desirability makes them targets of royal conscription, and pleasantness here describes human worth seized by force.
Saul is a handsome young man, the most impressive in appearance among all Israel — his pleasantness to the eye is a striking outward quality that sets him apart and makes him seem fit for leadership.

David has beautiful eyes and a handsome appearance — his pleasing outward quality accompanies God's choice of him, though the verse implicitly contrasts outer pleasantness with the inner heart God truly sees.
David's servants appeal to a feast day — the pleasantness here is the quality of a good day that makes generosity fitting and the inner mood of celebration a ground for the request.
David sees Bathsheba bathing and she is very beautiful — her visual pleasantness acts on his perception and stirs desire, setting in motion a chain of inner impulses that lead to moral failure.
David is called the sweet psalmist of Israel — his pleasantness is a quality of his inner spirit expressed through song, a God-given gift of lyrical beauty that delighted and edified the nation.
Joy is the collective inner state of the returned exiles as they celebrate the temple dedication — a communal, outwardly expressed gladness rooted in the restoration of worship.
The term here describes the quality of bronze vessels valued as precious as gold; it conveys material excellence rather than an inner-being state in this verse.
The term describes Vashti's physical loveliness — her pleasant appearance — which the king wanted displayed before the princes; the delight is aesthetic and external, not an inner state.
The term describes young women who are physically beautiful and pleasing; the pleasantness sought is outward attractiveness for the king's selection, not an inner-being quality.
The term again points to outward physical beauty — young women gathered for their pleasant appearance; the inner-being dimension is not directly in view in this administrative context.
Esther's pleasant appearance — beautiful figure and lovely to look at — is the focus; the term describes her external attractiveness rather than any inner emotional state.
Esther pleased Hegai and won his favor; the pleasantness here bridges outward appeal and the favorable inner response it produced in the one who perceived her.
The term here modifies Mordecai's word as one that 'saved the king' — describing his deed as good/beneficial; the pleasantness is moral-relational rather than an inner emotional state.
The term describes the holiday as a 'good' day — framing the Jews' gladness and feasting as a positively valued occasion; the inner experience of delight is expressed through communal celebration.

The rural Jews observe the fourteenth of Adar as a good/holiday day of gladness and feasting; the pleasant quality of the day is linked to communal joy and gift-giving rather than private inner experience.
Sorrow is structurally contrasted with gladness and mourning with a holiday; the term marks a reversal of inner state — from grief to joy — memorialized in communal feasting and generosity.
Honey is called good and sweet to the taste — the term here describes sensory pleasantness as a positive, enjoyable quality, grounding delight in physical experience.
The term describes large, beautiful houses soon to be desolate; the pleasantness is aesthetic and material, and its coming ruin underscores the fragility of human delight in fine possessions.
The precious oil is described as 'good/pleasant'; the term here marks valuable, desirable material wealth that Hezekiah proudly displayed, with no direct inner-being content in this verse.

Sweet cane from a distant land is described as pleasant, yet God declares the sacrifices not pleasing to him; the term highlights the contrast between outward pleasantness and genuine inner-directed acceptability before God.
The term describes the very good figs — their quality is excellent; this is a physical, evaluative use without direct inner-being content, setting up the theological comparison that follows.
God directs Jeremiah's perception to distinguish the good figs from the bad; the term evaluates quality as a vehicle for conveying divine discernment — no direct inner-being emotional content.
God regards the exiles from Judah as 'good' like the good figs — the term expresses divine favorable disposition toward them, reflecting God's inner attitude of acceptance and intended blessing.

Pure gold, once precious and good, has grown dim and changed; the term's past quality of the gold underscores what has been lost, evoking lamentation over the destruction of what was once pleasing and valued.
The vine was planted on good soil — favorable and productive ground; the term describes environmental quality rather than inner-being content in this allegorical verse.
The good pieces of meat — thigh and shoulder — are the choice portions placed in the pot; the term signals desirable quality in a culinary metaphor, with no direct inner-being content.

The best and choicest trees of Lebanon are described with this term; it conveys the superlative quality of what was once glorious, now cast down — the term underscores loss of excellent status rather than an inner state.

God promises to feed his flock with good pasture — abundant, nourishing, and restful grazing land; the term describes the quality of divine provision that produces rest and satisfaction in those cared for.

The young men who refused the king's food were better in appearance — the term describes their improved physical condition; the pleasantness here is visible, bodily flourishing rather than an inner emotional state.

The shade of the trees is described as pleasant/good, providing sensory comfort that draws people to sacrifice there; the appeal is outward and physical, indirectly enabling idolatrous behavior rather than reflecting an inner joyful state.

God's rich/precious treasures have been plundered into pagan temples; the term marks the high value of what was taken, not an inner emotional state, though the implied inner response is grief at the desecration.

Nineveh is personified as a graceful prostitute with deadly charms; the term describes an outwardly attractive pleasantness that is seductive and destructive, with the inner reality being betrayal rather than genuine delight.
